title: Brazilian Feijoada (Simplified Pressure-Cooker)

subs.name: rajma (kidney beans) works well here — the stew turns a slightly different colour but the flavour is excellent

ingredients.name: pork shoulder or belly, cut into 3 cm pieces

subs.name: beef or mutton pieces for a similar hearty stew in non-pork households
omit meat entirely and add an extra cup of beans plus a handful of soya chunks for a vegetarian feijoada

steps.text: Drain and rinse the soaked black beans. Season the pork pieces generously with salt, black pepper and cumin. Heat 2 tbsp oil in a pressure cooker on high flame and sear the pork in batches until browned on all sides, about 3–4 minutes per batch. Remove and set aside.

In the same pressure cooker, add 1 tbsp oil and cook the diced onion over medium heat for 5 minutes until golden. Add the minced garlic and stir for 1 minute. Add the tomatoes with a pinch of salt and cook for 3–4 minutes until they soften.

Return the seared pork to the pot. Add the drained black beans, bay leaves and 5 cups water. Stir well, close the pressure cooker lid and cook on medium flame for 8–9 whistles until both the beans and pork are completely tender.

Allow the pressure to release naturally. Open the lid — the stew should be thick and dark. If it looks too watery, simmer uncovered on medium heat for 10 minutes stirring occasionally until it thickens to a spoonable consistency. Remove the bay leaves.

Stir in the orange or lemon juice for brightness, taste and adjust salt. Serve over white rice with sliced spring onion and a wedge of lime on the side. Traditional accompaniments include sautéed collard greens (use spinach) and fried cassava or toasted cassava flour (farinha).

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
Follow the cooker manufacturer's fill, venting and opening instructions; never force the lid open under pressure.
critical_checks: Use an appropriate food thermometer and safe doneness guidance for the specific cut of meat.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: pork → beef or mutton pieces for a similar hearty stew in non-pork households; black-beans → rajma (kidney beans) works well here — the stew turns a slightly different colour but the flavour is excellent; pork → omit meat entirely and add an extra cup of beans plus a handful of soya chunks for a vegetarian feijoada (dietType must change to vegetarian)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: The pressure cooker is the ideal tool for this recipe — it cuts the bean and pork cooking time from 3 hours to 30 minutes. Black beans are increasingly available in Indian cities; rajma is a completely interchangeable substitute. Pork belly available from specialty butchers in Christian and tribal regions of India is the closest cut to what Brazilians use. The thick, savoury stew eaten over rice mirrors the experience of dal-rice in Indian cuisine.
